data.text:
INT: Tape three of Mark Verstandig. We were talking about your family traditions, the nature of Jewish culture in Mielec. Can we continue on from what you were saying last tape? Let's talk now about how did this Jewish tradition express itself in your home. 

MV: As I mentioned my home was a very orthodox home. It was a kosher home. And my mother used to wear a wig. And besides this, it was traditional. 

Let's say-- take a Friday night. We used to go to the synagogue-- mother and father, and-- and mother stayed home. Father and children and my brother and I went to the synagogue. When we came back from the synagogue, we always used to take with us a poor fellow who-- who-- who-- who didn't have where to go. That was a tradition. Again, we called it an orayach. We called him a guest. 

In the synagogues, there were still poor people, especially from-- from other towns. They used to travel from town to town, begging. And in the synagogue, he used to stand next to the door. And when wealthier people finished, they went home after the service, they used to take with them at least one poor fellow with them home to have dinner. 

And besides this, we had permanent people from town. We had the permanent poor men. This-- their-- their-- their children were artisans. I think they were [NON-ENGLISH]. They made-- they were called blacharz. I don't know how to translate it. 

INT: Tinsmith. 

MV: Tinsmith. They were tinsmiths, but they were poor. So their grandfather used to be our permanent guest for every Shabbat and for every yontif, for every-- for-- for every-- for-- for every holiday. He used to come, and he used to sit with us at the-- at the-- at the dinner-- the dinner table. 

Now, we used to come home, was a very, very orthodox and nice-- really a nice atmosphere. You see the candles on the table. The table was covered-- covered table. And there was-- was a candelabra. 

And we used to come home. And my father used to go first, open the door, and my mother and my sisters were waiting. And he used to greet them with Shabbat Shalom, good Shabbos, a hearty one. 

And then we used to say with us-- he used to sing a song [HEBREW]. Peace be with you. We bring you peace. Like a dance, we were around the table. And then the kiddush, a blessing of the wine. And it was really a very nice atmosphere-- a very nice atmosphere. This was a Friday night. 

Saturday morning, it was not as nice, because you went to the synagogue in the morning. And if we didn't tell you, that we are not allowed to eat before we were going to the synagogue, only a snack-- coffee and a biscuit. And you came home 12 o'clock. You're very hungry, Your kiddush was very short. It was the blessing of the wine. It was a very short one. 

And the-- the meals were not as good as Friday night, because on Friday night, the meals were fresh. You are not allowed to cook on Shabbat. But when the pots are on the stove, they can stay on the stove. So they stayed on the stove, and they were hot. On Saturday, everything was cold. The only thing which was-- which was warm enough was the so-called cholent. 

That was a stew, which day before you-- you-- you-- you sent it to the bakers. And the bakers put it into the oven for the night. The oven was still warm or hot from the baking from baking on Friday. And on Saturday, you came to collect. You went to this baker. You sent your servant to the baker. Every pot has-- has a name. And you brought in the so-called cholent. 

And the cholent-- sometimes it was good, and sometimes it wasn't. They used to say that the angel is cooking-- that the angel is cooking the-- the cholent. Sometimes it is good. And sometimes it's not good. 

So I didn't like it on Saturday. But that was the time. That was the way of life. 

And then there was the third meal, the so-called shaleshudes. And the third meal, that means after the evening prayer-- the first evening prayer, afternoon prayer-- Mincha-- you came home, and you had a third meal on Shabbat. That was [NON-ENGLISH]. That was obligatory. You had to do-- had to it. 

Beautiful was the [HEBREW]. That means the-- the meal on the end of Shabbat. We called it to say goodbye to the princess in Shabbat-- [HEBREW]. Yes, and that was a very nice-- it was a very nice meal. And people were coming and singing and spending this until 10 o'clock or 11 o'clock at night. 

So that was-- that was the Sabbath. During the-- during the week, now, everything was as I told. You're kosher, and kosher is not an easy thing to do. You have to-- we have to be very careful. 

And besides this, let's say the-- the education-- my education was a typical-- typical traditional orthodox education. 

INT: Could I just stop you there, if I may? Let's finish off the household, the standard of living. Did your family eat meat every day? 

MV: Did family? 

INT: Did your family eat meat every day? 

MV: My family, yes, almost. Almost, almost every day meat. But the main meal was midday, and not like here in the evening. The main-- it's this up till now, I think, in Poland's the same. But the main meal with meat is midday. And-- and before going to bed, I mean, the-- the so-called supper, the so-called supper is a very poor. It's just a piece of bread and something-- a snack-- a snack and you go to bed. So I think it's better. It's-- I think it's a much better arrangement. 

INT: Would that be typical for most of the Jewish people there, eating meat almost every day? 

MV: I wouldn't say that it would be typical, but if-- but for wealthier people, yes, for not wealthy people, not very much. Some people would-- will-- would eat-- well, they would eat only a piece of bread and with-- with butter and a piece of herring. That would be it. A sardine was a luxury. A sardine was a luxury. But the wealthier people had meat almost every day, unless they didn't want to. 

But that was only for the Jewish people. The other ones, they-- the-- the-- and-- and-- and-- and that was the way of life of the-- of the Christian people-- of the Christian Poles in town. 

But as far as the way of life, the standard of living in the villages is quite different. It was unbelievable. You know, in the morning, the farmhands and small farmers would eat the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. Really, I couldn't tell you how it was made, with a bit of-- a bit of milk and-- and cream-- and cream mixed with potato, with-- with-- with the potato mash. And it was quite-- and-- and a bit of-- a bit of herbs. It was quite tasty. I'm telling you, it was quite tasty. And a piece of bread-- that was the breakfast. 

And that was the lunch, the dinner-- potatoes. And that was the supper. Farmhands, small farmers, had meat twice a year-- for Easter and Christmas. Just I'm telling you. This is not a joke-- twice a year. For Christmas, they had chickens, but the chickens they also sell because they needed money to buy nails, to buy iron for their farms, iron products for their farm. And this was a monopoly of the-- of the large-- of the large industries, which were, again, these industries were in the hands of non-Jews. And the prices were very high. 

So they needed the money, whatever they had. Yes, sometimes for Christmas, for instance, they used to-- they used to kill a pig if they had to, if they have. And see, they-- and the killing a pig was quite a-- was quite a-- a treat. It was a whole ceremony. The neighbors came, and everybody-- everybody was taking part in it. So normally, the people in the villages had meat only twice a year-- once for Christmas, and once for Easter. 

INT: And the Gentiles in the city? 

MV: And the Gentiles in the city had no-- the Gentiles in the city ate the same as the Jews. The only thing's the Jews didn't eat pork, and they did. The Jews didn't eat bacon for breakfast. They did. Also bacon was not a breakfast meal in-- in-- for anybody-- for anybody in Poland. 

In Poland, the breakfast consisted of eggs, coffee, coffee, a piece of bread, or a bagel, or a-- or a roll. And that was the breakfast. But bacon and eggs was not used-- was-- was not-- I mean, it was not accepted by the Gentile-- by the Gentile population in Poland. But they ate meat every day, the same as the-- as the-- as-- as the Jews. The town people ate differently than-- than-- than the people in the villages-- in-- in-- in the small villages. 

INT: What about fruit and vegetables-- where they plentiful? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Were fruit and vegetables plentiful? 

MV: I don't think it was plentiful. But you had to sell this to buy little things what you needed. As I told you, a question of every farmer needs-- needs some iron to-- to-- for-- for-- for the wheels of the cart-- of the carts and for their-- and for their little machinery, whatever they had. But that was in the hands of monopolies, which were, again in the monopolies of the large, Polish Gentile industries. And for that, they had to pay a lot of money. 

INT: Was it quite common for people to eat fruit and vegetables in your town? 

MV: No. Not-- not very common. Not very common-- fruit and-- fruit and vegetables. But we usually-- it was common for the-- for-- for the farmers, for the peasant people. They eat a lot of fruit-- not fruit, but vegetables. Of course, fruit was-- fruit very-- quite expensive. 

INT: Where would you place-- when you were growing up, where would you place your family? Were they in the middle of the social class, or were they quite wealthy? 

MV: They were very wealthy. They were landowners, very wealthy, very wealthy, before-- even before-- when-- when in 19-- I was there in 1936. And that was after-- that was after the depression. the depression was over. The depression was over. 

And then there was a question of the so-called agricultural reform. The government wanted to buy out larger estates and to divide them among the smaller farmers, so that they should become owners of land. They should-- for farmhands or small farmers should enlarge their-- their-- should enlarge their holdings, because the population enlarged. The farmer who had, let us say, five hectares, and he had three sons. So each son later, when he died, each son has only 1 and 1/2 hectares. So they were very poor. 

This had to be-- this had to be-- this had to be solved. This problem had to be solved. So there was at-- at that time a project of Madagascar-- of Madagascar. They-- it was the Pilsudski regime-- wanted the Poles to emigrate to Madagascar because they were-- they didn't have land. The sons of the small farmers didn't have any more land. There was nothing to divide. 

So they want them to go to Madagascar. And to Madagascar it will be-- it will be a two-fold-- a two-fold profit for Poland. First of all it will solve the problem of land in Poland. The land over there is from next to nothing. The government will give them the land. The colonial government will give them the land. There was plenty of land over there. 

And secondly, when a lot of Poles will come to Madagascar, Poland will claim that Madagascar should be a Polish colony. And so Poland will become a colonial power-- a colonial power. But the Polish peasant leadership was against it. They claimed that Madagascar is an island inhabited by cannibals. And what the government want? The government want to send the Poles over there to-- to-- to-- a cursed island. Who knows what kinds of climate over there is, and so on and so on. 

Now, it didn't-- it-- it-- I mean, it-- it didn't get off the ground. As a matter of fact, a few years later-- a few years later, the same plan was elaborated by the Polish government, but about-- again about Madagascar, that we should send to Madagascar the Polish Jews. And how clever, and how mature the Polish ministers were? I'll tell you. 

Take this example. They sent a delegation to Paris to confer with Léon Blum and with Georges Mandel, the minister for colonies, both Jews. They say they want them, they want Léon Blum and Georges Mandel to give Madagascar to Poles so that the-- to Poland, so that Poland should be able to expel the Jews to Madagascar. Oh, surely, Léon Blum and Georges Mandel we wouldn't have a bar of it. 

So that was the question of when the-- but I don't remember exactly what-- what your question was. 

INT: Oh, we're going back to finishing off about living standards in those days. You made mention earlier about the fact that your family's house had running water, which was an exception. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: So what did most people do for running water? 

MV: They didn't have any. There was an industry. People used to travel, used to carry-- used to, you know, to-- to drag a barrel-- used to drag a barrel of water, pulling a barrel of water from the pump, which was outside the town, around the town, and selling buckets of water to households. And I still remember there was one who came back again from the United States-- a poor immigrant. He made enough money in the United States to buy a horse and a cart. And on this cart he had two or three barrels of water. And he was traveling around the houses and selling buckets of water and carrying the buckets of water into the house. This is how people used to have water. 

INT: What about the WC and the washing-- people washing themselves? 

MV: Ah, that was, again, from the water, which they was-- which they were buying. Now, wash-- this I would say, to do the washing was quite a-- was quite a-- an-- an industry. I mean, first of all, there were professional women who were washing-- who were doing the washing. 

There where-- they used to have, and they put in the kitchen large containers. They called them balia. How do-- would you-- how would you translate balia into English? 

INT: Tubs. 

MV: Tubs-- it's not exactly a tub, but you could-- as a matter of fact, people used it sometimes-- sometimes for a tub. So they used to have such tubs-- round one-- round tubs. And they used to be such-- such a piece of timber covered with corrugated iron. And on that corrugated iron, they used to wash. They used to put, let's say, a shirt. There was water with this-- they would put it with a shirt, and used to put some-- some soap on the shirt or whatever is going-- anything what is being washed and used to rub it. You can see it in pictures sometimes. The Indians and so on are doing it here along the river-- along the river. 

So this was the washing clothes once and two-- once, two-- about every fortnight or every month, there was to be this washing time. There were professional women coming to wash whatever thing could be washed. 

But again, you should-- you had to buy the water from the water carriers. They were called water carriers-- vaser treger-- water carriers. 

INT: And your house was also an exception. It had an internal WC. 

MV: An internal-- and we had a bathroom. And internal-- and internal, we had got the-- he had the upstairs two toilets and downstairs two toilets. 

INT: And what did the-- most of the other people do for-- 

MV: And by the way-- by the way, at-- in the beginning-- in the beginning, there was no kanalisation. There was no sewerage. There was no sewerage. So once every month or every two months, there used to be-- there were-- there were such large containers underneath downstairs, because it would run downstairs. And they used to come and clean it up. 

As a matter of fact, you know, the big town Lodz-- I think you are come from Lodz-- a metropolis of 600,000 people, they didn't have a sewerage. They just didn't have a sewerage. So there were professional people going around and-- during the night, and cleaning-- and cleaning the toilets, cleaning out the large containers underneath. 

INT: Were they Gentiles? 

MV: They were Gentiles. They were-- 

INT: And what did you call them? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: What were they called? 

MV: I don't remember. I don't remember how they were called. I don't remember if there were special names for them. [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember that there were special names for them. But later, in Mielec-- no, in Lodz never, but later in Mielec, they made the sewerage system work. I remember, they were digging this canals and so on, and there were-- there was sewerage in Melbourne-- in Mielec. 

INT: And were most people using kerosene light for lighting at home? 

MV: Yeah, for lighting, they were-- it long had been there. And when they had electricity, they didn't use kerosene. But when there was about-- before they had kerosene-- in Sadkowa Gora we still had kerosene. We didn't-- we-- we-- we never had electricity there. 

INT: And what about the traditions of the mikvah for the men and women? 

MV: Oh yes, there was every-- in every little town, there was a bath-- a steam bath, a proper Turkish steam bath. And matter of fact, some Gentiles, who had a sense of really going to a good steam bath, used to come there on Friday, because it worked only on Friday. 

And it worked in such a way-- it was a large hole. At one end of the hole was a stove covered with stones and heated up with hardwood, not with coals, not with coal, but with hardwood, with hardwood. And against it were benches, like in the amphitheater. Higher-- it got up higher, higher, higher. And on top of the-- of the stove, from the roof, hanging from the roof almost, was a barrel of water-- was a barrel of water. 

And from time to time, the men who were serving as the bath keeper used to turn this barrel upside down, and hot water was poured on these hot stones. And a wave of hot steam used to come against this-- against the benches. And immediately water was pumped up through this barrel through to-- to-- to this barrel again. 

I'm telling you, I used to go there since I was maybe five years old. And this is why I-- why I can stand up today to the hot sauna of 90 degrees. I used to take a bucket-- two buckets of ice cold water and a towel submerged in this water and put it on top. And used to walk up to the top bench. 

And there on the top bench, after one minute, you had to pour a whole bucket of cold, icy cold, water on your head. You could stand another minute and a half and do the same with the other bucket. And then you had this towel, which was submerged in this cold water before. You put the towel of cold water on your head, and you negotiated the stairs down. 

While on the second there was this-- the benches were very large, like here at least-- at least 40 centimeters, because otherwise, people could slip, so at least 40 centimeters-- maybe, maybe 50 centimeters. When I was a second-- the higher you came, the hotter it was. But then you came down. On the second bench, I had prepared there my little broom made out of birch twigs. 

And I lie down, and I pressed the hot air against my front. I lied down on my front, on my belly, and I asked my neighbor to give me a few-- a-- a-- a few hits with this-- with this broom on the back. And from there, I jumped into a cold mikvah. Or if it was winter, I used to run out and roll in the snow. And nothing happened to me. And nothing happened to anybody, because it was so hot. You can't imagine how hot it was when you walked up the highest-- when you walked up the highest bench. 

It came from the heat, from this stove heated up-- from the stones heated up with-- with-- with dry wood. 

INT: And they had a separate night for women? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Had a separate night for women? 

MV: It was just-- woman not-- woman didn't go to this. Woman went only-- came only to this large mikvah. There there were washing facilities. There were-- there were tubs. They used to-- could take a tub. And after that, they'd used to go in the mikvah. The mikvah was like a-- like a small-- a small, deep pool, but a very cold one. 

That was so-- that was so-- as a matter of fact, today people wonder-- I am not a young men, yet I can sit in a-- in a-- in a-- in a hot sauna for half an hour in [INAUDIBLE] recreation center. It's about 85 degrees or 90 degrees. And other people come in and run out after five minutes. But I'm used to it, because I started when I was young. 

And there were quite a few Gentiles coming there. But they could come only on Friday afternoon. That was the only day when this steam bath was working. 

INT: According to religious practice, did your mother use the mikvah as the only time she went to wash herself? 

MV: No, we had a-- she had a bathroom. We had a bathroom-- a proper bathroom. So you didn't need to use the mikvah. Besides this, you don't need-- you're not washing yourself in the mikvah. If you want to wash yourself, you take a bath in the tub. After the tub, you went to the mikvah. The mikvah is only for ritual purposes. You go there, and you submerge in the mikvah, but not to wash. You can't take soap to the mikvah. 

You jump in. You see, like-- what's his name-- John the Baptist. You see? He used to take somebody and throw them into the river. You baptize him, but you're not wash him. 

INT: What was the language used at home? 

MV: The language we use at home was either German or Yiddish, because my father spoke perfect German. And this is the reason that I studied the German. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
